speaker: interviewer
text: Tape six, interview with Aniko Friedman. Interviewer Tina Tito-- Friedberg, sorry. Interviewer Tina Tito. December 14, 1995. Would you please introduce your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Alan Friedberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr Friedberg, sorry for the mistake.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there something that you would like to say to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, during all of these interviews and conversations with Aniko, I keep thinking about-- I had family in Europe also. My father had a brother and a sister who had family. I never knew any of them. And these were my cousins. And they-- they all-- one day, we knew that they had disappeared. And so this is to me kind of striking also. Because it-- of course it hits home with Aniko. But it-- it hits home also, it gets me back to many years ago in the late 1930s when I was aware that these things started to go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Well, maybe that's--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Latvia, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Latvia.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Latvia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well thank you. And thank you for sharing your home with us and your experiences.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my parents' wedding photo taken in Budapest, I guess, in 1927.

speaker: interviewee
text: And your mother's full name? Ilona Benedek. And my father Ferenc, at that time, Ferenc Weisz. Thank you. Tell us about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This happens to be the only picture I have from my young age. I must be five or six years old here in some kind of ballet performance.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was taken about 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: In--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Debrecen-- in Debrecen.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the only picture I have left with my mother and father. I guess this must have been taken around 1933 or so in Debrecen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Tell us about the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is-- this is that photo I mentioned earlier about my mother. My father-- we had this taken together to send it to my father just a few months before we were taken away. On the right shoulder you see that pale spot? That's where my head was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my father had it removed and enlarged. And he had it always next to his bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Tell us about the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my uncle, Tibor Benedek, in Paris, who is photographed with a friend of his-- Miss [INAUDIBLE]. She used to be very famous at one time-- a friend of Chevalier's, a singer, dancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And because of him--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's why I went to Paris. And my mother knew also Miss [INAUDIBLE]. And she used to go to Paris and visit and stay at her house by Versailles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Please explain this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father in a coal mine when his invention was introduced. This is under Stalinism in Hungary. It's called a hydraulically marching mine machine. And it's-- it's still-- it's still used in Hungarian as well as all Russian coal mines.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what year was this invented?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I must have been maybe '54, '55.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fine. Thank you. Please tell us about the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mentioned earlier that Revlon-- actually Charles Revson, who was the owner of Revlon-- hired me to head-- to be the expert of-- the fragrance expert of Revlon. They were just going into the perfume business in New York. And this is why I stayed in America. And that was my first job. And this is-- they had a publicity picture made of me. And this is just a proof, an unretouched proof that they have given me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is around 1961.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in 1961.

speaker: interviewer
text: In New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Please tell us about the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, Anne.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a year ago this summer, past summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was right here in the back of-- behind the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here in Huntington--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was visiting here in Huntington, yes. She lives in Fort Lauderdale.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my darling only grandson, Alexandre. And this picture was taken about three weeks ago. He was just visiting and gave it to me. He is eight years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he is the son of?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he is the son of my darling son, who even though I cannot find a current picture of him, he is nothing to be ashamed of. He is a very good-looking and wonderful guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Please tell us about this book.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So since that reunion in Germany I was telling you about, they erected a museum in this-- on the site of the old [? Dynamit ?] [? Nobel ?] headquarters, which was a landmark building. And it really is a quite remarkable building. I was invited for the opening. And I was also commissioned to make the only sculpture there. And this is the catalog from that museum, which is called also a documentation and informational center. And this is the catalog with-- with a photo of my sculpture on the cover, which represents slave laborers there, the way they were-- like the two figures are like one. One is-- one is with a bowed head, humiliated, heavy in-- heavily-- heavy labor carrying the grenade. And the other one is-- is standing-- standing erect looking upward and letting a bird fly off, representing freedom and our hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in 1992, the museum opening on the book--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was in '93-- '93 or '94 even.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here, it says 1992--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's when I-- I recall-- the sculpture was commissioned. But it was a landmark building. It took a long time to-- to build it just so. So the-- it wasn't inaugurated until '94.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name here, you use your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my professional name is Aniko Veres-Fe, which Aniko Veres is my maiden name and Fe was added because of other things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fine. Please, tell us about this article.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the January 16, 1995 edition of magazine, which was an Auschwitz memorial edition that I happened open by chance. And I found myself in this picture, which represents us, part of the 1,000 Hungarian girls, on our way from Auschwitz to this German factory. And I happen to be the tall girl right on the crease.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Please, tell us about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a homemade photograph of the-- of a maquette-- of the only holocaust memorial that I ever designed. And I always believe just like I don't think that-- that words can convey-- convey the inexplicable, the-- the-- the human tragedy that was the Holocaust, I feel the same way about the ability of visual arts to do that. But this I see as preferably a larger than life size bronze-- what--

speaker: interviewer
text: Obelisk?

speaker: interviewee
text: --would call it? No. It's thin slab, like a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Slab.

speaker: interviewee
text: --slab, where the figures, the figure of a family, would be cut out. So it's of the void, the void that it represents in the lives, in the hearts of the survivors as well. And this is a side view, obviously. And the other part of the sculpture is that it would have to be on a large bare terrain. So the way the sun moves or reflectors, it always casts a shadow where the figures emerge in light. And I feel simplicity is the strongest. And that's the only thing that I would hope to one day to execute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.
